interactions.summary: Jiang explains why Pompey's containment strategy should have beaten Caesar, then identifies Caesar's two advantages: the world's best army and divisions inside the optimate-Pompey coalition.
Jiang tracks Caesar's offensive through Spain and Greece, emphasizing his un-Roman use of clemency and the optimate pressure that forces Pompey into battle at Pharsalus.
Jiang narrates Pharsalus, Caesar's later conquests, 'Vinni, vedi, vici,' and the breakdown of clemency when Caesar's aging veterans massacre enemies despite his orders.
Jiang closes the civil-war narrative with Caesar's powerful army, his return to Rome, reforms in land, debt, calendar, clemency, and citizenship, and the emergence of a cult of personality.

claims.claim: Pompey's best strategy is containment: because Pompey controls Rome's food provinces, he can wait for hunger, troop disloyalty, and Roman resentment to weaken Caesar.
Caesar faces one man versus an entire empire, but he has a highly disciplined, personally devoted army and opponents divided by distrust of Pompey's growing power.
Caesar's clemency after Spain is not very Roman in Jiang's framing: he releases defeated enemies if they promise not to fight him, though some go back to Pompey anyway.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretive military-political claim in this lecture.

claims.claim: At Pharsalus, optimate pressure overrides Pompey's preferred containment strategy, and Caesar's disciplined army withstands the cavalry hammer-and-anvil maneuver.
Jiang treats 'Vinni, vedi, vici' as Caesar's most famous line and another act of myth making that captures the Roman imagination.
Caesar's intended clemency collides with soldiers who want the war over; at one battle in Africa, veterans massacre enemies to ensure they cannot fight again.
By Munda, Jiang says Caesar's soldiers are so disciplined that they can attack uphill and destroy an enemy army, showing the extreme power of his force.

Caesar's reforms - land reform, debt relief, the Julian calendar, clemency, and expanded citizenship - are presented as efforts to resolve the contradictions that produced instability, civil war, and revolution.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretive reform summary in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: Caesar is one man versus an entire empire.
signature_moments.source_phrase: one man versus an entire empire
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The phrase compresses the apparent impossibility of Caesar's civil-war position.

signature_moments.moment: Caesar wins and then does something not very Roman: he lets enemies go.
signature_moments.source_phrase: he does something that's not very Roman
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Clemency becomes part of Caesar's new political myth and a challenge to older revenge norms.

signature_moments.moment: The most powerful Latin phrase captures Rome again: I came, I saw, I conquered.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I came, I saw, I conquered
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The line makes conquest feel instantaneous and theatrical, exactly the myth-maker's form.

signature_moments.moment: Caesar orders mercy, but his veterans massacre the enemy because they want the war finished.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Caesar orders them to show mercy the veterans his veterans massacred their enemies
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The moment shows the limit of Caesar's myth of clemency once war fatigue takes over.

signature_moments.moment: Caesar changes time itself by replacing Rome's lunar calendar with the Julian calendar.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Before Julius Caesar, the Romans used the lunar calendar
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Calendar reform makes identity disruption concrete: even the measure of time changes under Caesar.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Pompey's strategy of waiting Caesar out by using control of food provinces and time.
Caesar's policy of forgiving defeated enemies and inviting them back into Roman public life if they serve Rome.
A battle tactic where infantry locks the enemy in place and cavalry strikes from behind.
The August 48 BCE battle where Pompey and Caesar meet and Caesar's army defeats Pompey's strategy.
A calendar reform Caesar creates with astronomers to replace Rome's lunar calendar.
The public elevation of Caesar through omnipresent busts, coinage, and personal genius.

uncertainty_notes.note: The transcript says Caesar's army fought eight years in Spain; earlier context indicates Gaul. The semantic claim avoids depending on the location in that phrase.
Several battle names and phrases in the Africa passage appear noisy in ASR; claims preserve Jiang's broad point about clemency, soldiers, and massacre rather than canonical battle details.
